buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

# Daily price data
prices = pd.DataFrame({
    'date': pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=5),
    'price': [100, 101, 102, 103, 104]
})

# Earnings announcements (sparse events)
earnings = pd.DataFrame({
    'date': ['2020-01-02', '2020-01-04'],
    'eps_surprise': [0.05, -0.03]
})
earnings['date'] = pd.to_datetime(earnings['date'])

# Merge prices with NEAREST earnings
merged = pd.merge_asof(
    prices.sort_values('date'),
    earnings.sort_values('date'),
    on='date',
    direction='nearest'  # BUG: uses future earnings
)

signal = merged['eps_surprise'].fillna(0)
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

prices = pd.DataFrame({
    'date': pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=5),
    'price': [100, 101, 102, 103, 104]
})

earnings = pd.DataFrame({
    'date': ['2020-01-02', '2020-01-04'],
    'eps_surprise': [0.05, -0.03]
})
earnings['date'] = pd.to_datetime(earnings['date'])

# Use 'backward' to only use PAST earnings announcements
merged = pd.merge_asof(
    prices.sort_values('date'),
    earnings.sort_values('date'),
    on='date',
    direction='backward'  # Only use earnings already announced
)

signal = merged['eps_surprise'].fillna(0)
description: merge_asof with direction='nearest' matches each price date to the closest earnings date, which can be in the future.
explanation: direction='nearest' finds the closest earnings announcement whether it is before OR after the price date. On 2020-01-01 (before any earnings), this matches to the 2020-01-02 earnings release, which has not happened yet. The signal on 2020-01-01 is now based on tomorrow's earnings surprise — classic lookahead bias hidden inside a pandas merge operation.
suggested_fix: Always use direction='backward' in merge_asof when merging event data with price data. This ensures only past events are used.

issues.title: merge_asof with direction='nearest' uses future event data
